=== 隋書に記録された殯 === 『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」には、死者は棺槨を以って斂（おさ）め、親賓は屍に就いて歌舞し、妻子兄弟は白布を以って服を作る。貴人は3年外に殯し、庶人は日を卜してうずむ。「{{lang|zh|死者斂以棺槨親賓就屍歌舞妻子兄弟以白布製服 貴人三年殯於外庶人卜日而 及葬置屍船上陸地牽之}}」とあり、また、『隋書』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 高麗}}」（[[高句麗]]）には、死者は屋内に於て殯し、3年を経て、吉日を択（えら）んで葬る、父母夫の喪は3年服す「{{lang|zh|死者殯於屋内 經三年 擇吉日而葬 居父母及夫之喪 服皆三年 兄弟三月 初終哭泣 葬則鼓舞作樂以送之 埋訖 悉取死者生時服玩車馬置於墓側 會葬者爭取而去}}」とある。これらの記録から、[[倭国]]・高句麗とも、貴人は3年間殯にしたことがうかがえる。
なお、殯の終了後は棺を墳墓に[[埋葬]]した。長い殯の期間は[[墓|大規模な墳墓]]の整備に必要だったとも考えられる。
== 皇室における「殯」 == [[皇室喪儀令|皇室喪儀令第一章大喪儀第六條]]「[[大行天皇]][[太皇太后]][[皇太后]][[皇后]]ノ柩ハ之ヲ'''殯宮'''ニ奉遷ス」とあり、附式第一編大喪儀に詳しく方式が記されている。
殯宮は「もがりのみや」という名で[[天皇]]の[[大喪の礼]]に、また「ひんきゅう」という名で[[皇后]]・[[皇太后]]・[[太皇太后]]の[[斂葬の儀]]までの間、[[皇居]]宮殿内に仮設される遺体安置所の名として使用されることになっており、戦後に於いては[[昭和天皇]]や[[貞明皇后]]、[[香淳皇后]]の崩御の際に設置されている。
死後13日目に遺体を収めた柩は御所の櫬殿（しんでん）から宮殿内の殯宮（ひんきゅう）に移御され、45日目を目処に行われる[[大喪の礼]]や[[斂葬の儀]]までの間に、[[通夜]]にあたる諸儀式「'''殯宮祗候'''（ひんきゅうしこう）」が行われる。
* 殯宮祗候 ** 殯宮移御の儀 ** 殯宮日供の儀（[[※]] 毎日行われる。） ** 殯宮移御後一日祭の儀 ** 殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 殯宮二十日の儀 ** 殯宮三十日の儀 ** 殯宮四十日の儀 ** 斂葬前殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 斂葬当日殯宮祭の儀
[[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）[[8月8日]]、第125代[[天皇]]（現・[[明仁|上皇]]）による「[[象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]]」で殯についての言及があった。
{{Quotation|更にこれまでの皇室のしきたりとして，天皇の終焉に当たっては，重い'''殯'''の行事が連日ほぼ2ヶ月にわたって続き，その後喪儀に関連する行事が，1年間続きます。その様々な行事と，新時代に関わる諸行事が同時に進行することから，行事に関わる人々，とりわけ残される家族は，非常に厳しい状況下に置かれざるを得ません。|明仁|<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば] {{ja icon}}</ref>}}
これに先立つ[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[11月15日]]、天皇と皇后は宮内庁を通じ「自身の葬礼に[[火葬]]を希望する」旨を表明している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/life/news/131115/lif1311150009-n1.html 【両陛下ご葬送 火葬に】歴代の天皇、3分の1は火葬] {{リンク切れ|date= 2023年6月12日|title= サイト上に「 ページが見つかりませんでした。 」と表記されている。（記事削除 または 記事リンク無効）}} - [[産経新聞]]、2013年（平成25年）11月15日配信（2023年（令和5年）6月12日 [[閲覧]]）</ref>。この場合、これまでよりも短期の殯宮祗候を斎行したのち、[[密葬]]にあたる葬送儀礼を経て火葬する。霊櫃（[[遺骨]]を納めた[[骨壷]]）を殯宮と同じ場所に設えた「奉安宮（ほうあんきゅう）」に安置し、本葬（斂葬の儀）や[[国葬]]・大喪の礼を待つこととなる。
== 殯の衰退 == 殯の儀式は[[大化の改新]]以降に出された[[薄葬令]]によって、葬儀の簡素化や墳墓の小型化が進められた結果、[[仏教]]とともに[[日本]]に伝わったと言われる[[火葬]]の普及もあり、急速に衰退する。
[[青森県]]の一部の[[地域]]では忌中の家の前に1.8メートルくらいの長さの木の棒を2本交差させて、X印にしたものを門に飾る地域もある。殯の風習が形を変えて残っているものと思われる。
== 現代に生きる殯の名残 == [[通夜]]は殯の[[風習]]の名残で、殯の期間が1日だけ、あるいは数日だけに短縮されたものとする説もある。[[沖縄県|沖縄]]でかつては広く行われ、[[現代 (時代区分)|現代]]でも一部の[[離島]]に残る[[風葬]]と[[洗骨]]の風習は、殯の一種の形態と考えられる。
== 関連項目 == * [[鶴見山古墳]] - 「殯」の痕跡が確認されている。 * [[九相図|九相観]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{葬制}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:もかり}}
[[Category:葬儀]]

'''殯'''（もがり）とは[[日本]]の[[葬送儀礼]]。古代において行われて、[[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）[[現在]]では、[[天皇]]・[[皇后]]・[[太皇太后]]・[[皇太后]]の[[大喪儀]]において行われる。
死者を[[埋葬]]するまでの長い期間、[[死体|遺体]]を納棺して仮安置する。期間は数ヶ月ないし数年に及ぶ。儀式を執り行う者は、死者との別れを惜しみ、死者の[[霊魂]]を畏れかつ慰め、死者の復活を願いつつも、遺体の腐敗・[[白骨化]]などの物理的変化を確認することにより、'''死者の最終的な「[[死]]」を確認する。'''
'''殯において棺'''を安置する場所をも「'''殯」と呼ぶことがある。'''また、遺体を安置した建物を「'''殯宮'''」（「もがりのみや」、『万葉集』では「あらきのみや」）という。
== 概要 == === 日本の古文書にみる殯 === 『[[古事記]]』・『[[日本書紀]]』では殯、『[[万葉集]]』では大殯とされ、貴人を殯にした記録や、それを連想させる記録が散見されるが、具体的な方法などは[[記録]]されていない。
『日本書紀』においては、一書の九で[[イザナギ]]が[[イザナミ]]の腐乱した遺骸を見た際「'''伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處'''」の殯斂や[[天稚彦]]（あめわかひこ）の殯「'''便造喪屋而殯之'''」（一書の一「'''而於天作喪屋殯哭之'''」）、巻8の[[仲哀天皇]]の死後にその遺体を[[武内宿禰]]による海路に穴門を通って豊浦宮におけるもの「'''竊收天皇之屍 付武内宿禰 以從海路遷穴門 而殯于豐浦宮 爲无火殯斂'''<small>'''无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利'''</small>」があり、その後数代して[[欽明天皇]]（欽明天皇32年[[4月15日 (旧暦)|4月15日]]（[[571年]][[5月24日]]）[[崩御]]）32年5月に河内古市に殯し、秋8月に[[新羅]]の[[未叱子失消]]が殯に哀悼した「'''五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月[[丙子]][[朔]] 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯 是月 未叱子失消等罷 九月 葬于檜隈阪合陵'''」と記述される。なおこのときは1年に満たない殯である。
[[平安時代]][[wikt:中期|中期]]の[[貴族]][[藤原行成]]の[[日記]]『[[権記]]』[[寛弘]]元年（[[1004年]]）三月十三日の条に、[[村上天皇]]の皇子女の殯処に関する記述があり、「'''此くのごとき処、皆、荒る'''」とあるところから、村上天皇の御代後の平安中期頃に殯を行う対象者の範囲や規模が縮小したと推察される。
=== 隋書に記録された殯 === 『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」には、死者は棺槨を以って斂（おさ）め、親賓は屍に就いて歌舞し、妻子兄弟は白布を以って服を作る。貴人は3年外に殯し、庶人は日を卜してうずむ。「{{lang|zh|死者斂以棺槨親賓就屍歌舞妻子兄弟以白布製服 貴人三年殯於外庶人卜日而 及葬置屍船上陸地牽之}}」とあり、また、『隋書』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 高麗}}」（[[高句麗]]）には、死者は屋内に於て殯し、3年を経て、吉日を択（えら）んで葬る、父母夫の喪は3年服す「{{lang|zh|死者殯於屋内 經三年 擇吉日而葬 居父母及夫之喪 服皆三年 兄弟三月 初終哭泣 葬則鼓舞作樂以送之 埋訖 悉取死者生時服玩車馬置於墓側 會葬者爭取而去}}」とある。これらの記録から、[[倭国]]・高句麗とも、貴人は3年間殯にしたことがうかがえる。
なお、殯の終了後は棺を墳墓に[[埋葬]]した。長い殯の期間は[[墓|大規模な墳墓]]の整備に必要だったとも考えられる。
== 皇室における「殯」 == [[皇室喪儀令|皇室喪儀令第一章大喪儀第六條]]「[[大行天皇]][[太皇太后]][[皇太后]][[皇后]]ノ柩ハ之ヲ'''殯宮'''ニ奉遷ス」とあり、附式第一編大喪儀に詳しく方式が記されている。
殯宮は「もがりのみや」という名で[[天皇]]の[[大喪の礼]]に、また「ひんきゅう」という名で[[皇后]]・[[皇太后]]・[[太皇太后]]の[[斂葬の儀]]までの間、[[皇居]]宮殿内に仮設される遺体安置所の名として使用されることになっており、戦後に於いては[[昭和天皇]]や[[貞明皇后]]、[[香淳皇后]]の崩御の際に設置されている。
死後13日目に遺体を収めた柩は御所の櫬殿（しんでん）から宮殿内の殯宮（ひんきゅう）に移御され、45日目を目処に行われる[[大喪の礼]]や[[斂葬の儀]]までの間に、[[通夜]]にあたる諸儀式「'''殯宮祗候'''（ひんきゅうしこう）」が行われる。
* 殯宮祗候 ** 殯宮移御の儀 ** 殯宮日供の儀（[[※]] 毎日行われる。） ** 殯宮移御後一日祭の儀 ** 殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 殯宮二十日の儀 ** 殯宮三十日の儀 ** 殯宮四十日の儀 ** 斂葬前殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 斂葬当日殯宮祭の儀
[[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）[[8月8日]]、第125代[[天皇]]（現・[[明仁|上皇]]）による「[[象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]]」で殯についての言及があった。
{{Quotation|更にこれまでの皇室のしきたりとして，天皇の終焉に当たっては，重い'''殯'''の行事が連日ほぼ2ヶ月にわたって続き，その後喪儀に関連する行事が，1年間続きます。その様々な行事と，新時代に関わる諸行事が同時に進行することから，行事に関わる人々，とりわけ残される家族は，非常に厳しい状況下に置かれざるを得ません。|明仁|<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば] {{ja icon}}</ref>}}
これに先立つ[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[11月15日]]、天皇と皇后は宮内庁を通じ「自身の葬礼に[[火葬]]を希望する」旨を表明している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/life/news/131115/lif1311150009-n1.html 【両陛下ご葬送 火葬に】歴代の天皇、3分の1は火葬] {{リンク切れ|date= 2023年6月12日|title= サイト上に「 ページが見つかりませんでした。 」と表記されている。（記事削除 または 記事リンク無効）}} - [[産経新聞]]、2013年（平成25年）11月15日配信（2023年（令和5年）6月12日 [[閲覧]]）</ref>。この場合、これまでよりも短期の殯宮祗候を斎行したのち、[[密葬]]にあたる葬送儀礼を経て火葬する。霊櫃（[[遺骨]]を納めた[[骨壷]]）を殯宮と同じ場所に設えた「奉安宮（ほうあんきゅう）」に安置し、本葬（斂葬の儀）や[[国葬]]・大喪の礼を待つこととなる。
== 殯の衰退 == [[大化の改新]]以降に出された[[薄葬令]]によって葬儀の簡素化や墳墓の小型化が進められた。また[[火葬]]が普及した。このような葬送の変化に伴い殯は廃れ、現代では皇室を除いて殯が行われることはほぼ無い。
== 現代に生きる殯の名残 == [[通夜]]は殯の[[風習]]の名残で、殯の期間が短縮されたものとする説もある。
[[沖縄県|沖縄]]でかつては[[風葬]]と[[洗骨]]が広く行われ、[[現代 (時代区分)|現代]]でも一部の[[離島]]に残っているが、これらは殯の一種と考えられる。
[[青森県]]の一部の[[地域]]では忌中の家の前に1.8メートルくらいの長さの木の棒を2本交差させてX印にしたものを門に飾るが、殯の風習が形を変えて残っているものと思われる。

殯宮は「もがりのみや」という名で[[天皇]]の[[大喪の礼]]に、また「ひんきゅう」という名で[[皇后]]・[[皇太后]]・[[太皇太后]]の[[斂葬の儀]]までの間、[[皇居]]宮殿内に仮設される遺体安置所の名として使用されることになっており、戦後に於いては[[昭和天皇]]や[[貞明皇后]]、[[香淳皇后]]の崩御の際に設置されている。
死後13日目に遺体を収めた柩は御所の櫬殿（しんでん）から宮殿内の殯宮（ひんきゅう）に移御され、45日目を目処に行われる[[大喪の礼]]や[[斂葬の儀]]までの間に、[[通夜]]にあたる諸儀式「'''殯宮祗候'''（ひんきゅうしこう）」が行われる。
* 殯宮祗候 ** 殯宮移御の儀 ** 殯宮日供の儀（[[※]] 毎日行われる。） ** 殯宮移御後一日祭の儀 ** 殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 殯宮二十日の儀 ** 殯宮三十日の儀 ** 殯宮四十日の儀 ** 斂葬前殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 斂葬当日殯宮祭の儀
[[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）[[8月8日]]、第125代[[天皇]]（現・[[明仁|上皇]]）による「[[象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]]」で殯についての言及があった。
{{Quotation|更にこれまでの皇室のしきたりとして，天皇の終焉に当たっては，重い'''殯'''の行事が連日ほぼ2ヶ月にわたって続き，その後喪儀に関連する行事が，1年間続きます。その様々な行事と，新時代に関わる諸行事が同時に進行することから，行事に関わる人々，とりわけ残される家族は，非常に厳しい状況下に置かれざるを得ません。|明仁|<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば] {{ja icon}}</ref>}}
これに先立つ[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[11月15日]]、天皇と皇后は宮内庁を通じ「自身の葬礼に[[火葬]]を希望する」旨を表明している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/life/news/131115/lif1311150009-n1.html 【両陛下ご葬送 火葬に】歴代の天皇、3分の1は火葬] {{リンク切れ|date= 2023年6月12日|title= サイト上に「 ページが見つかりませんでした。 」と表記されている。（記事削除 または 記事リンク無効）}} - [[産経新聞]]、2013年（平成25年）11月15日配信（2023年（令和5年）6月12日 [[閲覧]]）</ref>。この場合、これまでよりも短期の殯宮祗候を斎行したのち、[[密葬]]にあたる葬送儀礼を経て火葬する。霊櫃（[[遺骨]]を納めた[[骨壷]]）を殯宮と同じ場所に設えた「奉安宮（ほうあんきゅう）」に安置し、本葬（斂葬の儀）や[[国葬]]・大喪の礼を待つこととなる。
== 殯の衰退 == [[大化の改新]]以降に出された[[薄葬令]]によって葬儀の簡素化や墳墓の小型化が進められた。また[[火葬]]が普及した。このような葬送の変化に伴い殯は廃れ、現代では皇室を除いて殯が行われることはほぼ無い。
== 現代に生きる殯の名残 == [[通夜]]は殯の[[風習]]の名残で、殯の期間が短縮されたものとする説もある。
[[沖縄県|沖縄]]でかつては[[風葬]]と[[洗骨]]が広く行われ、[[現代 (時代区分)|現代]]でも一部の[[離島]]に残っているが、これらは殯の一種と考えられる。
[[青森県]]の一部の[[地域]]では忌中の家の前に1.8メートルくらいの長さの木の棒を2本交差させてX印にしたものを門に飾るが、殯の風習が形を変えて残っているものと思われる。
== 関連項目 == * [[鶴見山古墳]] - 「殯」の痕跡が確認されている。 * [[九相図|九相観]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{葬制}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:もかり}}
[[Category:葬儀]]

'''殯'''（もがり）とは[[日本]]の[[葬送儀礼]]。古代において行われ、[[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）[[現在]]では、[[天皇]]・[[皇后]]・[[太皇太后]]・[[皇太后]]の[[大喪儀]]において行われる。
死者を[[埋葬]]するまでの長い期間、[[死体|遺体]]を納棺して仮安置する。期間は数ヶ月ないし数年に及ぶ。儀式を執り行う者は、死者との別れを惜しみ、死者の[[霊魂]]を畏れかつ慰め、死者の復活を願いつつも、遺体の腐敗・[[白骨化]]などの物理的変化を確認することにより死者の最終的な[[死]]を確認する。
殯において棺を安置する場所をも「殯」と呼ぶことがある。また、遺体を安置した建物を「殯宮」（「もがりのみや」、『万葉集』では「あらきのみや」）という。
== 概要 == === 日本の古文書にみる殯 === 『[[古事記]]』・『[[日本書紀]]』では殯、『[[万葉集]]』では大殯とされ、貴人を殯にした記録や、それを連想させる記録が散見されるが、具体的な方法などは[[記録]]されていない。
『日本書紀』においては、一書の九で[[イザナギ]]が[[イザナミ]]の腐乱した遺骸を見た際「'''伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處'''」の殯斂や[[天稚彦]]（あめわかひこ）の殯「'''便造喪屋而殯之'''」（一書の一「'''而於天作喪屋殯哭之'''」）、巻8の[[仲哀天皇]]の死後にその遺体を[[武内宿禰]]による海路に穴門を通って豊浦宮におけるもの「'''竊收天皇之屍 付武内宿禰 以從海路遷穴門 而殯于豐浦宮 爲无火殯斂'''<small>'''无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利'''</small>」があり、その後数代して[[欽明天皇]]（欽明天皇32年[[4月15日 (旧暦)|4月15日]]（[[571年]][[5月24日]]）[[崩御]]）32年5月に河内古市に殯し、秋8月に[[新羅]]の[[未叱子失消]]が殯に哀悼した「'''五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月[[丙子]][[朔]] 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯 是月 未叱子失消等罷 九月 葬于檜隈阪合陵'''」と記述される。なおこのときは1年に満たない殯である。
[[平安時代]][[wikt:中期|中期]]の[[貴族]][[藤原行成]]の[[日記]]『[[権記]]』[[寛弘]]元年（[[1004年]]）三月十三日の条に、[[村上天皇]]の皇子女の殯処に関する記述があり、「'''此くのごとき処、皆、荒る'''」とあるところから、村上天皇の御代後の平安中期頃に殯を行う対象者の範囲や規模が縮小したと推察される。
=== 隋書に記録された殯 === 『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」には、死者は棺槨を以って斂（おさ）め、親賓は屍に就いて歌舞し、妻子兄弟は白布を以って服を作る。貴人は3年外に殯し、庶人は日を卜してうずむ。「{{lang|zh|死者斂以棺槨親賓就屍歌舞妻子兄弟以白布製服 貴人三年殯於外庶人卜日而 及葬置屍船上陸地牽之}}」とあり、また、『隋書』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 高麗}}」（[[高句麗]]）には、死者は屋内に於て殯し、3年を経て、吉日を択（えら）んで葬る、父母夫の喪は3年服す「{{lang|zh|死者殯於屋内 經三年 擇吉日而葬 居父母及夫之喪 服皆三年 兄弟三月 初終哭泣 葬則鼓舞作樂以送之 埋訖 悉取死者生時服玩車馬置於墓側 會葬者爭取而去}}」とある。これらの記録から、[[倭国]]・高句麗とも、貴人は3年間殯にしたことがうかがえる。
なお、殯の終了後は棺を墳墓に[[埋葬]]した。長い殯の期間は[[墓|大規模な墳墓]]の整備に必要だったとも考えられる。
== 皇室における「殯」 == [[皇室喪儀令|皇室喪儀令第一章大喪儀第六條]]「[[大行天皇]][[太皇太后]][[皇太后]][[皇后]]ノ柩ハ之ヲ'''殯宮'''ニ奉遷ス」とあり、附式第一編大喪儀に詳しく方式が記されている。
殯宮は「もがりのみや」という名で[[天皇]]の[[大喪の礼]]に、また「ひんきゅう」という名で[[皇后]]・[[皇太后]]・[[太皇太后]]の[[斂葬の儀]]までの間、[[皇居]]宮殿内に仮設される遺体安置所の名として使用されることになっており、戦後に於いては[[昭和天皇]]や[[貞明皇后]]、[[香淳皇后]]の崩御の際に設置されている。
死後13日目に遺体を収めた柩は御所の櫬殿（しんでん）から宮殿内の殯宮（ひんきゅう）に移御され、45日目を目処に行われる[[大喪の礼]]や[[斂葬の儀]]までの間に、[[通夜]]にあたる諸儀式「'''殯宮祗候'''（ひんきゅうしこう）」が行われる。
* 殯宮祗候 ** 殯宮移御の儀 ** 殯宮日供の儀（[[※]] 毎日行われる。） ** 殯宮移御後一日祭の儀 ** 殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 殯宮二十日の儀 ** 殯宮三十日の儀 ** 殯宮四十日の儀 ** 斂葬前殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 斂葬当日殯宮祭の儀
[[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）[[8月8日]]、第125代[[天皇]]（現・[[明仁|上皇]]）による「[[象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]]」で殯についての言及があった。
{{Quotation|更にこれまでの皇室のしきたりとして，天皇の終焉に当たっては，重い'''殯'''の行事が連日ほぼ2ヶ月にわたって続き，その後喪儀に関連する行事が，1年間続きます。その様々な行事と，新時代に関わる諸行事が同時に進行することから，行事に関わる人々，とりわけ残される家族は，非常に厳しい状況下に置かれざるを得ません。|明仁|<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば] {{ja icon}}</ref>}}
これに先立つ[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[11月15日]]、天皇と皇后は宮内庁を通じ「自身の葬礼に[[火葬]]を希望する」旨を表明している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/life/news/131115/lif1311150009-n1.html 【両陛下ご葬送 火葬に】歴代の天皇、3分の1は火葬] {{リンク切れ|date= 2023年6月12日|title= サイト上に「 ページが見つかりませんでした。 」と表記されている。（記事削除 または 記事リンク無効）}} - [[産経新聞]]、2013年（平成25年）11月15日配信（2023年（令和5年）6月12日 [[閲覧]]）</ref>。この場合、これまでよりも短期の殯宮祗候を斎行したのち、[[密葬]]にあたる葬送儀礼を経て火葬する。霊櫃（[[遺骨]]を納めた[[骨壷]]）を殯宮と同じ場所に設えた「奉安宮（ほうあんきゅう）」に安置し、本葬（斂葬の儀）や[[国葬]]・大喪の礼を待つこととなる。
== 殯の衰退 == [[大化の改新]]以降に出された[[薄葬令]]によって葬儀の簡素化や墳墓の小型化が進められた。また[[火葬]]が普及した。このような葬送の変化に伴い殯は廃れ、現代では皇室を除いて殯が行われることはほぼ無い。
== 現代に生きる殯の名残 == [[通夜]]は殯の[[風習]]の名残で、殯の期間が短縮されたものとする説もある。
[[沖縄県|沖縄]]でかつては[[風葬]]と[[洗骨]]が広く行われ、[[現代 (時代区分)|現代]]でも一部の[[離島]]に残っているが、これらは殯の一種と考えられる。
[[青森県]]の一部の[[地域]]では忌中の家の前に1.8メートルくらいの長さの木の棒を2本交差させてX印にしたものを門に飾るが、殯の風習が形を変えて残っているものと思われる。
== 関連項目 == * [[鶴見山古墳]] - 「殯」の痕跡が確認されている。 * [[九相図|九相観]]

revision.comment: 過剰なリンク、特に必要の無い強調を整理
revision.text:
'''殯'''（もがり）とは、[[日本]]の古代に行われていた[[葬送儀礼]]。死者を[[埋葬]]するまでの長い期間、遺体を納棺して仮安置し、別れを惜しみ、死者の霊魂を畏れ、かつ慰め、死者の復活を願いつつも遺体の腐敗・[[白骨化]]などの物理的変化を確認することにより、死者の最終的な「[[死]]」を確認すること。その柩を安置する場所をも指すことがある。殯の期間に遺体を安置した建物を「'''殯宮'''」（「もがりのみや」、『万葉集』では「あらきのみや」）という。
現在では、[[天皇]]・[[皇后]]・[[太皇太后]]・[[皇太后]]の[[大喪儀]]の一つとして行われる。
== 概要 == === 日本の古文書にみる殯 === 『[[古事記]]』、『[[日本書紀]]』では殯、『[[万葉集]]』では大殯とされ、貴人を殯にした記録や、それを連想させる記録が散見されるが、具体的な方法などは記録されていない。
『日本書紀』においては、一書の九で[[イザナギ]]が[[イザナミ]]の腐乱した遺骸を見た際「伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處」の殯斂や[[天稚彦]]（あめわかひこ）の殯「便造喪屋而殯之」（一書の一「而於天作喪屋殯哭之」）、巻8の[[仲哀天皇]]の死後にその遺体を[[武内宿禰]]による海路に穴門を通って豊浦宮におけるもの「竊收天皇之屍 付武内宿禰 以從海路遷穴門 而殯于豐浦宮 爲无火殯斂<small>无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利</small>」があり、その後数代して[[欽明天皇]]（欽明天皇32年[[4月15日 (旧暦)|4月15日]]（[[571年]][[5月24日]]）死去）32年5月に河内古市に殯し、秋8月に[[新羅]]の未叱子失消が殯に哀悼した「五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月[[丙子]][[朔]] 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯 是月 未叱子失消等罷 九月 葬于檜隈阪合陵」と記述される。なおこのときは1年に満たない殯である。
平安中期の貴族[[藤原行成]]の日記『[[権記]]』寛弘元年（1004年）三月十三日の条に、[[村上天皇]]の皇子女の殯処に関する記述があり、「此くのごとき処、皆、荒る」とあるところから、村上天皇の御代後の平安中期頃に殯を行う対象者の範囲や規模が縮小したと推察される。
=== 隋書に記録された殯 === 『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」には、死者は棺槨を以って斂（おさ）め、親賓は屍に就いて歌舞し、妻子兄弟は白布を以って服を作る。貴人は3年外に殯し、庶人は日を卜してうずむ。「{{lang|zh|死者斂以棺槨親賓就屍歌舞妻子兄弟以白布製服 貴人三年殯於外庶人卜日而 及葬置屍船上陸地牽之}}」とあり、また、『隋書』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 高麗}}」（[[高句麗]]）には、死者は屋内に於て殯し、3年を経て、吉日を択（えら）んで葬る、父母夫の喪は3年服す「{{lang|zh|死者殯於屋内 經三年 擇吉日而葬 居父母及夫之喪 服皆三年 兄弟三月 初終哭泣 葬則鼓舞作樂以送之 埋訖 悉取死者生時服玩車馬置於墓側 會葬者爭取而去}}」とある。これらの記録から、[[倭国]]・高句麗とも、貴人は3年間殯にしたことがうかがえる。
なお、殯の終了後は棺を墳墓に[[埋葬]]した。長い殯の期間は大規模な墳墓の整備に必要だったとも考えられる。
== 皇室における「殯」 == [[皇室喪儀令|皇室喪儀令第一章大喪儀第六條]]「[[大行天皇]][[太皇太后]][[皇太后]][[皇后]]ノ柩ハ之ヲ'''殯宮'''ニ奉遷ス」とあり、附式第一編大喪儀に詳しく方式が記されている。
殯宮は「もがりのみや」という名で[[天皇]]の[[大喪の礼]]に、また「ひんきゅう」という名で[[皇后]]・[[皇太后]]・[[太皇太后]]の[[斂葬の儀]]までの間、[[皇居]]宮殿内に仮設される遺体安置所の名として使用されることになっており、戦後に於いては[[昭和天皇]]や[[貞明皇后]]、[[香淳皇后]]の崩御の際に設置されている。
死後13日目に遺体を収めた柩は御所の櫬殿（しんでん）から宮殿内の殯宮（ひんきゅう）に移御され、45日目を目処に行われる[[大喪の礼]]や[[斂葬の儀]]までの間に、[[通夜]]にあたる諸儀式「'''殯宮祗候'''（ひんきゅうしこう）」が行われる。 * 殯宮祗候 ** 殯宮移御の儀 ** 殯宮日供の儀（毎日行われる） ** 殯宮移御後一日祭の儀 ** 殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 殯宮二十日の儀 ** 殯宮三十日の儀 ** 殯宮四十日の儀 ** 斂葬前殯宮拝礼の儀 ** 斂葬当日殯宮祭の儀 [[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）[[8月8日]]、第125代[[天皇]]（現・[[上皇明仁|上皇]]）による「[[象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]]」で殯についての言及があった。
{{Quotation|更にこれまでの皇室のしきたりとして，天皇の終焉に当たっては，重い'''殯'''の行事が連日ほぼ2ヶ月にわたって続き，その後喪儀に関連する行事が，1年間続きます。その様々な行事と，新時代に関わる諸行事が同時に進行することから，行事に関わる人々，とりわけ残される家族は，非常に厳しい状況下に置かれざるを得ません。|明仁|<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 象徴としてのお務めについての天皇陛下のおことば]</ref>}}
これに先立つ[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[11月15日]]、天皇と皇后は宮内庁を通じ「自身の葬礼に火葬を希望する」旨を表明している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/life/news/131115/lif1311150009-n1.html 【両陛下ご葬送 火葬に】歴代の天皇、3分の1は火葬] - [[産経新聞]]、2013年11月15日配信（2019年5月28日閲覧）</ref>。この場合、これまでよりも短期の殯宮祗候を斎行したのち、[[密葬]]にあたる葬送儀礼を経て火葬する。霊櫃（遺骨を納めた骨壷）を殯宮と同じ場所に設えた「奉安宮（ほうあんきゅう）」に安置し、本葬・斂葬の儀や国葬・大喪の礼を待つこととなる。
== 殯の衰退 == 殯の儀式は[[大化の改新]]以降に出された[[薄葬令]]によって、葬儀の簡素化や墳墓の小型化が進められた結果、[[仏教]]とともに日本に伝わったと言われる[[火葬]]の普及もあり、急速に衰退する。 青森県の一部の地域では忌中の家の前に1.8メートルくらいの長さの木の棒を2本交差させて、X印にしたものを門に飾る地域もある。殯の風習が形を変えて残っているものと思われる。
== 現代に生きる殯の名残 == [[通夜]]は殯の風習の名残で、殯の期間が1日だけ、あるいは数日だけに短縮されたものとする説もある。[[沖縄県|沖縄]]でかつては広く行われ、現代でも一部の離島に残る[[風葬]]と[[洗骨]]の風習は、殯の一種の形態と考えられる。
== 関連項目 == * [[鶴見山古墳]] - 「殯」の痕跡が確認されている * [[九相図|九相観]]
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
{{葬制}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もかり}} [[Category:葬儀]]

During this extended period, the deceased's body was placed in a temporary coffin and enshrined in a provisional site, while loved ones mourned, revered the soul, and performed rituals to comfort the spirit and confirm its passing by observing the physical decomposition and [[skeletonization]]. The term can also refer to the location where the coffin was enshrined. Buildings..."
revision.text:
'''Mogari''' (殯) refers to a [[funeral rite]] in ancient [[Japan]] conducted before [[burial]]. During this extended period, the deceased's body was placed in a temporary coffin and enshrined in a provisional site, while loved ones mourned, revered the soul, and performed rituals to comfort the spirit and confirm its passing by observing the physical decomposition and [[skeletonization]]. The term can also refer to the location where the coffin was enshrined. Buildings used to house the body during this period were called "'''Mogari no Miya'''” (殯宮, "Temporary Palace of Mourning") and referred to as "Araki no Miya" in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''.
Today, it is performed as part of the [[Great Funeral Rites]] for [[emperors]], [[empresses]], [[grand empress dowagers]], and [[empress dowagers]].
== Overview == === Mogari in Japanese Historical Documents === In texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', mogari is mentioned, while in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' it is referred to as ''Ōmogari'' (大殯). Records of noble individuals undergoing mogari or references suggesting such practices exist, but specific methods are not detailed.
The ''Nihon Shoki'' describes instances such as [[Izanagi]] observing the decayed remains of [[Izanami]] ("伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處") and the mogari of [[Ame no Wakahiko]] ("便造喪屋而殯之"). It also records the mogari of [[Chūai Tennō]] at Toyoura Palace, where his body was temporarily enshrined and described as "无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利." An entry for [[Kinmei Tennō]] states that his body underwent mogari for less than a year: "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子朔 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯."
The ''[[Gonki]]'' diary of [[Fujiwara no Yukinari]] in the Heian period mentions deteriorated mogari sites, suggesting that by this era, the scope and scale of mogari practices had diminished.
=== Mogari in the ''Sui Shu'' === The ''[[Book of Sui]]'' describes mogari in ''Eastern Barbarians' Biographies'' (俀國 and 高麗). It states that nobles conducted mogari for three years, with rituals involving singing and dancing near the body. After this period, the deceased were buried on an auspicious date. In both [[Wa (Japan)]] and [[Goguryeo]], mogari lasted for three years for nobles.
After the mogari period ended, the coffin was buried in a tomb. It is believed that this long period allowed for the preparation of elaborate burial mounds.
== Imperial Mogari == The [[Imperial Household Funeral Order]] (皇室喪儀令) stipulated the practice of mogari for [[emperors]] and [[empresses]]. For example, the order states: "The coffin of the Emperor, Empress, Grand Empress Dowager, or Empress Dowager shall be transferred to the Mogari Palace (殯宮)." In practice, the ''Mogari Palace'' (もがりのみや) is established within the Imperial Palace after the [[Shinden]] phase and serves as a temporary enshrinement site.
=== Modern Practice === During the [[Showa Emperor]] and [[Empress Kojun]]'s reigns, the mogari ceremony (殯宮祗候) included: * Transfer to the ''Mogari Palace'' * Daily offerings * Special rites marking the 20th, 30th, and 40th days * Final funeral and burial rites
On August 8, 2016, [[Emperor Akihito]] referenced mogari during his address about his abdication concerns, highlighting its extended duration and ceremonial significance.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 Emperor Akihito's address on his role as a symbol]</ref>
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari practices declined after the [[Taika Reform]] introduced burial simplifications. The [[Thin Burial Edict]] and the spread of [[cremation]] also contributed to its decline. However, traces remain in customs such as [[wakes]] and regional burial rites.
== Legacy of Mogari == Modern [[wakes]] are believed to be a shortened version of mogari. In regions like [[Okinawa]], practices of [[sky burials]] and [[bone washing]] retain elements of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} – Traces of mogari have been identified here. * {{ill|Kusozu|ja|九相図}} – Artistic depictions of corpse decay stages.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

After the mogari period ended, the coffin was buried in a tomb. It is believed that this long period allowed for the preparation of elaborate burial mounds.
== Imperial Mogari == The [[Imperial Household Funeral Order]] (皇室喪儀令) stipulated the practice of mogari for [[emperors]] and [[empresses]]. For example, the order states: "The coffin of the Emperor, Empress, Grand Empress Dowager, or Empress Dowager shall be transferred to the Mogari Palace (殯宮)." In practice, the ''Mogari Palace'' (もがりのみや) is established within the Imperial Palace after the [[Shinden]] phase and serves as a temporary enshrinement site.
=== Modern Practice === During the [[Showa Emperor]] and [[Empress Kojun]]'s reigns, the mogari ceremony (殯宮祗候) included: * Transfer to the ''Mogari Palace'' * Daily offerings * Special rites marking the 20th, 30th, and 40th days * Final funeral and burial rites
On August 8, 2016, [[Emperor Akihito]] referenced mogari during his address about his abdication concerns, highlighting its extended duration and ceremonial significance.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 Emperor Akihito's address on his role as a symbol]</ref>
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari practices declined after the [[Taika Reform]] introduced burial simplifications. The [[Thin Burial Edict]] and the spread of [[cremation]] also contributed to its decline. However, traces remain in customs such as [[wakes]] and regional burial rites.
== Legacy of Mogari == Modern [[wakes]] are believed to be a shortened version of mogari. In regions like [[Okinawa]], practices of [[sky burials]] and [[bone washing]] retain elements of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} – Traces of mogari have been identified here. * {{ill|Kusozu|ja|九相図}} – Artistic depictions of corpse decay stages.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral}} [[Category:Last 100]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Funerals]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:殯]]

'''Mogari''' (殯) refers to a [[funeral rite]] in ancient [[Japan]] conducted before [[burial]]. During this extended period, the deceased's body was placed in a temporary coffin and enshrined in a provisional site, while loved ones mourned, revered the soul, and performed rituals to comfort the spirit and confirm its passing by observing the physical decomposition and [[skeletonization]]. The term can also refer to the location where the coffin was enshrined. Buildings used to house the body during this period were called "'''Mogari no Miya'''” (殯宮, "Temporary Palace of Mourning") and referred to as "Araki no Miya" in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''.
Today, it is performed as part of the [[Great Funeral Rites]] for [[emperors]], [[empresses]], [[grand empress dowagers]], and [[empress dowagers]].
== Overview == === Mogari in Japanese Historical Documents === In texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', mogari is mentioned, while in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' it is referred to as ''Ōmogari'' (大殯). Records of noble individuals undergoing mogari or references suggesting such practices exist, but specific methods are not detailed.
The ''Nihon Shoki'' describes instances such as [[Izanagi]] observing the decayed remains of [[Izanami]] ("伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處") and the mogari of [[Ame no Wakahiko]] ("便造喪屋而殯之"). It also records the mogari of [[Chūai Tennō]] at Toyoura Palace, where his body was temporarily enshrined and described as "无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利." An entry for [[Kinmei Tennō]] states that his body underwent mogari for less than a year: "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子朔 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯."
The ''[[Gonki]]'' diary of [[Fujiwara no Yukinari]] in the Heian period mentions deteriorated mogari sites, suggesting that by this era, the scope and scale of mogari practices had diminished.
=== Mogari in the ''Sui Shu'' === The ''[[Book of Sui]]'' describes mogari in ''Eastern Barbarians' Biographies'' (俀國 and 高麗). It states that nobles conducted mogari for three years, with rituals involving singing and dancing near the body. After this period, the deceased were buried on an auspicious date. In both [[Wa (Japan)]] and [[Goguryeo]], mogari lasted for three years for nobles.
After the mogari period ended, the coffin was buried in a tomb. It is believed that this long period allowed for the preparation of elaborate burial mounds.
== Imperial Mogari == The [[Imperial Household Funeral Order]] (皇室喪儀令) stipulated the practice of mogari for [[emperors]] and [[empresses]]. For example, the order states: "The coffin of the Emperor, Empress, Grand Empress Dowager, or Empress Dowager shall be transferred to the Mogari Palace (殯宮)." In practice, the ''Mogari Palace'' (もがりのみや) is established within the Imperial Palace after the [[Shinden]] phase and serves as a temporary enshrinement site.
=== Modern Practice === During the [[Showa Emperor]] and [[Empress Kojun]]'s reigns, the mogari ceremony (殯宮祗候) included: * Transfer to the ''Mogari Palace'' * Daily offerings * Special rites marking the 20th, 30th, and 40th days * Final funeral and burial rites
On August 8, 2016, [[Emperor Akihito]] referenced mogari during his address about his abdication concerns, highlighting its extended duration and ceremonial significance.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 Emperor Akihito's address on his role as a symbol]</ref>
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari practices declined after the [[Taika Reform]] introduced burial simplifications. The [[Thin Burial Edict]] and the spread of [[cremation]] also contributed to its decline. However, traces remain in customs such as [[wakes]] and regional burial rites.
== Legacy of Mogari == Modern [[wakes]] are believed to be a shortened version of mogari. In regions like [[Okinawa]], practices of [[sky burials]] and [[bone washing]] retain elements of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} – Traces of mogari have been identified here. * {{ill|Kusozu|ja|九相図}} – Artistic depictions of corpse decay stages.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

After the mogari period ended, the coffin was buried in a tomb. It is believed that this long period allowed for the preparation of elaborate burial mounds.
== Imperial Mogari == The [[Imperial Household Funeral Order]] (皇室喪儀令) stipulated the practice of mogari for [[emperors]] and [[empresses]]. For example, the order states: "The coffin of the Emperor, Empress, Grand Empress Dowager, or Empress Dowager shall be transferred to the Mogari Palace (殯宮)." In practice, the ''Mogari Palace'' (もがりのみや) is established within the Imperial Palace after the [[Shinden]] phase and serves as a temporary enshrinement site.
=== Modern Practice === During the [[Showa Emperor]] and [[Empress Kojun]]'s reigns, the mogari ceremony (殯宮祗候) included: * Transfer to the ''Mogari Palace'' * Daily offerings * Special rites marking the 20th, 30th, and 40th days * Final funeral and burial rites
On August 8, 2016, [[Emperor Akihito]] referenced mogari during his address about his abdication concerns, highlighting its extended duration and ceremonial significance.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 Emperor Akihito's address on his role as a symbol]</ref>
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari practices declined after the [[Taika Reform]] introduced burial simplifications. The [[Thin Burial Edict]] and the spread of [[cremation]] also contributed to its decline. However, traces remain in customs such as [[wakes]] and regional burial rites.
== Legacy of Mogari == Modern [[wakes]] are believed to be a shortened version of mogari. In regions like [[Okinawa]], practices of [[sky burials]] and [[bone washing]] retain elements of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} – Traces of mogari have been identified here. * {{ill|Kusozu|ja|九相図}} – Artistic depictions of corpse decay stages.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral}} [[Category:Last 100]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Funerals]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:殯]]

'''Mogari''' (殯) refers to a [[funeral rite]] in ancient [[Japan]] conducted before [[burial]]. During this extended period, the deceased's body was placed in a temporary coffin and enshrined in a provisional site, while loved ones mourned, revered the soul, and performed rituals to comfort the spirit and confirm its passing by observing the physical decomposition and [[skeletonization]]. The term can also refer to the location where the coffin was enshrined. Buildings used to house the body during this period were called "'''Mogari no Miya'''” (殯宮, "Temporary Palace of Mourning") and referred to as "Araki no Miya" in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''.
Today, it is performed as part of the [[Great Funeral Rites]] for [[emperors]], [[empresses]], [[grand empress dowagers]], and [[empress dowagers]].
== Overview == === Mogari in Japanese Historical Documents === In texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', mogari is mentioned, while in the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' it is referred to as ''Ōmogari'' (大殯). Records of noble individuals undergoing mogari or references suggesting such practices exist, but specific methods are not detailed.
The ''Nihon Shoki'' describes instances such as [[Izanagi]] observing the decayed remains of [[Izanami]] ("伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處") and the mogari of [[Ame no Wakahiko]] ("便造喪屋而殯之"). It also records the mogari of [[Chūai Tennō]] at Toyoura Palace, where his body was temporarily enshrined and described as "无火殯斂 此謂褒那之阿餓利." An entry for [[Kinmei Tennō]] states that his body underwent mogari for less than a year: "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子朔 新羅遣弔使 未叱子失消等 奉哀於殯."
The ''[[Gonki]]'' diary of [[Fujiwara no Yukinari]] in the Heian period mentions deteriorated mogari sites, suggesting that by this era, the scope and scale of mogari practices had diminished.
=== Mogari in the ''Sui Shu'' === The ''[[Book of Sui]]'' describes mogari in ''Eastern Barbarians' Biographies'' (俀國 and 高麗). It states that nobles conducted mogari for three years, with rituals involving singing and dancing near the body. After this period, the deceased were buried on an auspicious date. In both [[Wa (Japan)]] and [[Goguryeo]], mogari lasted for three years for nobles.
After the mogari period ended, the coffin was buried in a tomb. It is believed that this long period allowed for the preparation of elaborate burial mounds.
== Imperial Mogari == The [[Imperial Household Funeral Order]] (皇室喪儀令) stipulated the practice of mogari for [[emperors]] and [[empresses]]. For example, the order states: "The coffin of the Emperor, Empress, Grand Empress Dowager, or Empress Dowager shall be transferred to the Mogari Palace (殯宮)." In practice, the ''Mogari Palace'' (もがりのみや) is established within the Imperial Palace after the [[Shinden]] phase and serves as a temporary enshrinement site.
=== Modern Practice === During the [[Showa Emperor]] and [[Empress Kojun]]'s reigns, the mogari ceremony (殯宮祗候) included: * Transfer to the ''Mogari Palace'' * Daily offerings * Special rites marking the 20th, 30th, and 40th days * Final funeral and burial rites
On August 8, 2016, [[Emperor Akihito]] referenced mogari during his address about his abdication concerns, highlighting its extended duration and ceremonial significance.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/page/okotoba/detail/12 Emperor Akihito's address on his role as a symbol]</ref>
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari practices declined after the [[Taika Reform]] introduced burial simplifications. The [[Thin Burial Edict]] and the spread of [[cremation]] also contributed to its decline. However, traces remain in customs such as [[wakes]] and regional burial rites.
== Legacy of Mogari == Modern [[wakes]] are believed to be a shortened version of mogari. In regions like [[Okinawa]], practices of [[sky burials]] and [[bone washing]] retain elements of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} – Traces of mogari have been identified here. * {{ill|Kusozu|ja|九相図}} – Artistic depictions of corpse decay stages.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Funerals]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Mogari]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:殯]]

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}

{{Funeral Practices}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Funerals]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mogari]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:殯]]

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."

== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.

Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.

The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.

Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*

The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) * *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* * *Daily Ritual Offerings* * *First Day After Transfer Ritual* * *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* * *20th Day Ceremony* * *30th Day Ceremony* * *40th Day Ceremony* * *Ritual Before Renso* * *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) ** *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* ** *Daily Ritual Offerings* ** *First Day After Transfer Ritual* ** *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* ** *20th Day Ceremony* ** *30th Day Ceremony* ** *40th Day Ceremony* ** *Ritual Before Renso* ** *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari. == See Also! == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) ** *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* ** *Daily Ritual Offerings* ** *First Day After Transfer Ritual* ** *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* ** *20th Day Ceremony* ** *30th Day Ceremony* ** *40th Day Ceremony* ** *Ritual Before Renso* ** *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=draft}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=draft}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=draft}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨|12=draft}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=draft}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図|12=draft}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:100 oldest pages]] [[Category:Funerals]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[en:Mogari]] [[ja:殯]] [[sn:Mogari]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Mogari}}

'''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}* as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the *{{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=draft}}* for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}* and *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}*, mogari is mentioned, while the *{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}* refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the *Nihon Shoki*, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦|12=draft}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The *Nihon Shoki* mentions Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=draft}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇|12=draft}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書|12=draft}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=draft}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) ** *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* ** *Daily Ritual Offerings* ** *First Day After Transfer Ritual* ** *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* ** *20th Day Ceremony* ** *30th Day Ceremony* ** *40th Day Ceremony* ** *Ritual Before Renso* ** *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=draft}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=draft}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=draft}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨|12=draft}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=draft}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図|12=draft}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

The *Nihon Shoki* also records Emperor {{ill|Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, *{{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=draft}}*, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇|12=draft}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the *Book of Sui* === The *{{ill|Book of Sui|ja|隋書|12=draft}}* describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The *{{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=draft}}*, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the *Taihō no Rei* or *Renso no Gi* ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * *Mogari-no-miya Shikō* (殯宮祗候) ** *Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya* ** *Daily Ritual Offerings* ** *First Day After Transfer Ritual* ** *Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony* ** *20th Day Ceremony* ** *30th Day Ceremony* ** *40th Day Ceremony* ** *Ritual Before Renso* ** *Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day*
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the *{{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=draft}}* issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the *{{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=draft}}* (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as *{{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=draft}}* and *{{ill|Senbotsu|ja|洗骨|12=draft}}* (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=draft}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine Stages of Decay|ja|九相図|12=draft}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral Practices}}
{{citation needed|Mogari}}
