<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] '''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the {{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōgi|1=Taihōgi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433650}} for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], mogari is mentioned, while the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the Nihon Shoki, various examples appear: * When [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] witnesses the decayed corpse of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame Wakahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノワカヒコ|1=Ame no Wakahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940674}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The Nihon Shoki mentions Emperor [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Chūai]]’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The Nihon Shoki also records Emperor [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Kinmei]] undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, {{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gonki|1=Gonki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542341}}, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]]'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the Book of Sui === The [[:en:Book of Sui|Book of Sui]] describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the Taihō no Rei or Renso no Gi ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]], Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the {{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsōrei|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}} issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the {{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsūya|1=Tsūya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q99899230}} (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as {{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fūsō|1=wind burial|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665579}} and [[:en:Bone collecting|Senbotsu]] (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurumiyama Kofun|qq=}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * [[:en:Nine stages of decay|Nine Stages of Decay]]
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral Practices}}
{{translated page|ja|殯|version=101564289|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| }}

The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the {{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōgi|1=Taihōgi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433650}} for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, mogari is mentioned, while the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the Nihon Shoki, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame Wakahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノワカヒコ|1=Ame no Wakahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940674}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The Nihon Shoki mentions Emperor {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Chūai}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The Nihon Shoki also records Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Kinmei}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, {{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gonki|1=Gonki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542341}}, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the Book of Sui === The {{ill|Book of Sui|en|Book of Sui|WD=Q1156924|lt=Book of Sui}} describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the Taihō no Rei or Renso no Gi ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Shōwa}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the {{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsōrei|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}} issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the {{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsūya|1=Tsūya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q99899230}} (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as {{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fūsō|1=wind burial|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665579}} and {{ill|Bone collecting|en|Bone collecting|WD=Q11077654|lt=Senbotsu}} (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurumiyama Kofun|qq=}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine stages of decay|en|Nine stages of decay|WD=Q11370419|lt=Nine Stages of Decay}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral Practices}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] '''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the {{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōgi|1=Taihōgi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433650}} for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, mogari is mentioned, while the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} refers to it as "Ōmogari." The Nihon Shoki also records Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Kinmei}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, {{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gonki|1=Gonki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542341}}, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the Book of Sui === The {{ill|Book of Sui|en|Book of Sui|WD=Q1156924|lt=Book of Sui}} describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] '''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the {{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōgi|1=Taihōgi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433650}} for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, mogari is mentioned, while the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}} refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the Nihon Shoki, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame Wakahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノワカヒコ|1=Ame no Wakahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940674}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The Nihon Shoki mentions Emperor {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Chūai}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The Nihon Shoki also records Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Kinmei}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, {{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gonki|1=Gonki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542341}}, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the Book of Sui === The {{ill|Book of Sui|en|Book of Sui|WD=Q1156924|lt=Book of Sui}} describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the Taihō no Rei or Renso no Gi ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Shōwa}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the {{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsōrei|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}} issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the {{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsūya|1=Tsūya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q99899230}} (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as {{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fūsō|1=wind burial|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665579}} and {{ill|Bone collecting|en|Bone collecting|WD=Q11077654|lt=Senbotsu}} (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurumiyama Kofun|qq=}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine stages of decay|en|Nine stages of decay|WD=Q11370419|lt=Nine Stages of Decay}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral Practices}}
{{translated page|ja|殯|version=101564289|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the Taihō no Rei or Renso no Gi ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Shōwa|1=Hirohito}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the {{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsōrei|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}} issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the {{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsūya|1=Q99899230|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q99899230}} (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as {{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fūsō|1=wind burial|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665579}} and {{ill|Bone collecting|en|Bone collecting|WD=Q11077654|lt=Senbotsu|1=Bone collecting}} (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurumiyama Kofun|qq=}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine stages of decay|en|Nine stages of decay|WD=Q11370419|lt=Nine Stages of Decay|1=Nine stages of decay}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:殯]] '''Mogari''' (殯) is an ancient Japanese funeral rite. It refers to the prolonged period before burial when the deceased's body is placed in a coffin for temporary enshrinement. During this time, people mourn the loss, fear and appease the spirit of the deceased, and hope for the deceased's revival while confirming the physical changes of decay and skeletonization to affirm their final "death." The term also refers to the location where the coffin is placed. The building used for temporary enshrinement during the mogari period is called a "'''Mogari-no-miya'''" (殯宮), referred to in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} as "Araki-no-miya."
Today, this ritual is conducted as part of the {{ill|Taihōgi|ja|大喪儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōgi|1=Q11433650|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433650}} for the Emperor, Empress, Empress Dowager, and Grand Empress Dowager.
== Overview == === Mogari in Ancient Japanese Records === In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, mogari is mentioned, while the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} refers to it as "Ōmogari." Records describe noble individuals undergoing mogari or practices reminiscent of it, though specific methods are not detailed.
In the Nihon Shoki, various examples appear: * When {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} witnesses the decayed corpse of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, it states, "伊弉諾尊欲見其妹 乃到殯斂之處," referencing mogari. * Regarding the mogari of {{ill|Ame Wakahiko|ja|天稚彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame Wakahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノワカヒコ|1=Ame no Wakahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940674}}, it is said, "便造喪屋而殯之" (一書の一). * The Nihon Shoki mentions Emperor {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}}’s body undergoing mogari at Toyoura Palace under Takenouchi no Sukune's care, described as "無火殯斂."
The Nihon Shoki also records Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} undergoing mogari for less than a year after his death in 571 CE, noting, "五月 殯于河內古市 秋八月 丙子 朔."
The diary of court noble Fujiwara no Yukinari, {{ill|Gonki|ja|権記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gonki|1=Gonki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542341}}, contains a record from March 13, 1004, mentioning the mogari of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}}'s children, suggesting that by the mid-Heian period, the scope and scale of mogari ceremonies had diminished.
=== Mogari in the Book of Sui === The {{ill|Book of Sui|en|Book of Sui|WD=Q1156924|lt=Book of Sui|1=Book of Sui}} describes funeral customs in Wa (Japan), stating: "The deceased is placed in a coffin. Relatives and guests sing and dance by the body, while immediate family members wear white mourning clothes. Nobles keep the body in mogari for three years, while commoners bury it after choosing an auspicious day."
Similarly, the section on Goguryeo mentions: "The deceased is enshrined in the house for three years before burial. During this time, mourning rituals, including music and dance, are performed."
These accounts imply that the practice of mogari for nobles persisted for up to three years in both Wa and Goguryeo.
== Mogari in the Imperial Family == The {{ill|Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|ja|皇室喪儀令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|1=Imperial Household Mourning Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581035}}, Article 6, states: "The coffins of the Emperor, Grand Empress Dowager, Empress Dowager, and Empress shall be placed in the 'Mogari-no-miya.'" Detailed procedures are provided in the ordinance's appendix.
The mogari-no-miya is a temporary structure built within the Imperial Palace to house the body until the Taihō no Rei or Renso no Gi ceremonies. In modern times, such structures were established for Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Shōwa|1=Hirohito}}, Empress Teimei, and Empress Kōjun.
During the mourning period, the following rituals are conducted: * Mogari-no-miya Shikō (殯宮祗候) ** Ritual for Transfer to the Mogari-no-miya ** Daily Ritual Offerings ** First Day After Transfer Ritual ** Mogari-no-miya Worship Ceremony ** 20th Day Ceremony ** 30th Day Ceremony ** 40th Day Ceremony ** Ritual Before Renso ** Mogari-no-miya Ceremony on Renso Day
In 2016, Emperor Emeritus Akihito referenced mogari in his address, noting its demanding nature on the bereaved family and others involved.
== Decline of Mogari == Mogari ceremonies declined after the {{ill|Sōsōrei|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsōrei|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}} issued during the Taika Reforms, which promoted simpler funerals and smaller tombs. The spread of cremation, introduced alongside Buddhism, also contributed to its decline.
== Legacy of Mogari in Modern Times == * The custom of the {{ill|Tsūya|ja|通夜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsūya|1=Q99899230|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q99899230}} (vigil) is considered a remnant of mogari. * In Okinawa, traditions such as {{ill|fūsō|ja|風葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fūsō|1=wind burial|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665579}} and {{ill|Bone collecting|en|Bone collecting|WD=Q11077654|lt=Senbotsu|1=Bone collecting}} (bone washing) are thought to represent forms of mogari.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsurumiyama Kofun|ja|鶴見山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurumiyama Kofun|qq=}} - Evidence of mogari has been found here. * {{ill|Nine stages of decay|en|Nine stages of decay|WD=Q11370419|lt=Nine Stages of Decay|1=Nine stages of decay}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
{{Funeral Practices}}
{{translated page|ja|殯|version=101564289|comment=Imported full ja history}}
